one_line_takeaway: A position paper arguing that LLM-code security should shift from post-hoc AI-based checking/attacking and human-gated neurosymbolic verification toward enforcing hard security constraints directly during code generation, especially via constrained decoding in diffusion-style (non-autoregressive) code models -- an untested proposal, not an evaluated system.
key_finding: The paper's own distinctive claim (not an empirical result) is the thesis that non-autoregressive/diffusion code generation is structurally better suited than autoregressive generation for secure-by-construction enforcement, because (i) autoregressive decoding cannot evaluate whole-program security properties from a partial prefix, while diffusion models generate/refine the entire program and thus permit constraint checking and enforcement over the complete program and at AST/module/function granularity, and (ii) this is tractable in practice because -- per the cited PatchDB dataset -- most real-world security fixes (buffer overruns, double frees, XSS, path traversal) are localized to one or two lines within a single function, making local, per-node constrained decoding a plausible mechanism for a general secure-by-construction guarantee. No implementation or evaluation of this proposal is presented in the paper.
core_contribution: This is a position/opinion paper (Livshits, Imperial College London, Feb 2026), not an empirical study. Its own argument (Sections 1-3.3) makes three claims: (1) the prevailing 'fighting fire with fire' pattern -- using probabilistic AI checkers or AI attackers to find/fix vulnerabilities in AI-generated code -- is fundamentally limited because it optimizes coverage under a fixed budget while real vulnerabilities hide in rare, exceptional execution paths (error handling, corner-case parsing, race windows), so 'not found' only means 'not reached,' not 'absent'; (2) neurosymbolic verification approaches (illustrated via two cited systems, Astrogator and ARC) are attractive but hard to reconcile with the 'vibe coding' workflow, because unless the end-to-end verification pipeline is fully automated, the human-in-the-loop becomes the weak link (developers habitually 'accept all changes'); and (3) the author's own proposed direction: obtain stronger guarantees by enforcing hard, non-probabilistic security constraints during code generation itself (e.g., constrained decoding) rather than relying on post-hoc detection/repair. The paper argues this is especially promising for diffusion-style (non-autoregressive) code models, because generating the whole program at once (rather than token-by-token) allows constraint evaluation/enforcement over the complete program, and because most security fixes appear to be structurally local (a 'Small World Hypothesis for Security Fixes,' invoking the cited PatchDB dataset's observation that most vulnerability fixes are 1-2 lines and contained within a single function).
threat_model: Informal, not formalized as assets/adversary capabilities/attack surface. The paper frames security work via an 'AI security triangle' of three roles: generator (the LLM/SLM producing code), checker (an LLM or static-analysis tool that analyzes the code), and attacker (an LLM- or human-driven exploit-finding process, e.g. pentesting/CTF-style). Its informal adversary is a better-resourced or more persistent attacker (stronger model, more compute/hardware, private models unavailable to defenders, or domain insight) who can search the vulnerability space defenders' automated attackers did not reach. The paper also notes a 'lack of forward security': code produced today may be broken by tomorrow's stronger models, and vice versa. No formal adversary model, asset list, or attack-surface enumeration is given -- this is argumentative framing, not a specified threat model.
limitations: The paper is explicitly a position piece with zero original experiments, benchmarks, or quantitative validation of its own proposal (diffusion-model constrained decoding for security). Its central recommendation is therefore untested by the author. All quantitative figures that appear in the text (e.g., prefix tuning raising secure-code rate from 59% to 92%, Astrogator's 83%/92% verification rates, ARC's >99% soundness with 15.6% recall) are results reported in cited third-party papers, not findings of this paper, and the review deliberately does not attribute them to this paper's own contribution. The 'Small World Hypothesis for Security Fixes' is asserted based on a single cited dataset (PatchDB) and is not independently validated by the author. No formal threat model, adversary capability bound, or systematic literature-review methodology is disclosed for how the ~35+ related works discussed in Section 4 were selected.
